meta.notes: Synthetic data; replace via Ollama or manual edits.

questions.text: Moral rightness depends solely on consequences.
questions.info: Consequentialism says an action's rightness depends entirely on its outcomes, often measured by overall happiness or welfare. Opponents argue some acts are wrong regardless of consequences (e.g., rights violations).

questions.text: There are objective moral truths independent of culture.
questions.info: Moral realism holds that moral truths exist independently of what anyone thinks. Anti‑realists claim moral judgments reflect attitudes, conventions, or emotions rather than facts.

questions.text: Free will is compatible with a deterministic universe.
questions.info: Compatibilism says free will can coexist with determinism: you act freely when actions flow from your own desires and reasons. Libertarians about free will and hard determinists reject this for opposite reasons.

questions.text: The external world exists independently of our perceptions.
questions.info: Realism about the external world claims objects exist independently of our perceptions. Skeptics and idealists challenge whether we can know, or whether existence depends on minds.

questions.text: All knowledge ultimately comes from sensory experience.
questions.info: Empiricism makes sensory experience the primary source of knowledge. Rationalists reply that reason alone yields substantive truths not derived from experience.

questions.text: Reason alone can yield substantive knowledge about reality.
questions.info: Rationalism maintains that some substantive knowledge about reality is knowable a priori by reason. Examples often include mathematics and certain metaphysical principles.

questions.text: Personal identity persists over time despite psychological change.
questions.info: Debates about personal identity weigh psychological continuity, bodily continuity, and memory. Some argue identity persists through change; others claim only relations of continuity matter, not strict identity.

questions.text: Mental states are nothing over and above physical states.
questions.info: Physicalism holds that mental states are nothing over and above physical states of the brain. Dualists and others argue consciousness resists purely physical explanation.

questions.text: Mathematical entities exist independently of human minds.
questions.info: Mathematical Platonism treats numbers and other entities as real and mind‑independent. Nominalists and formalists deny this, viewing mathematics as symbolic or conceptual.

questions.text: Scientific theories aim at truth, not merely usefulness.
questions.info: Scientific realism says successful theories aim at (approximate) truth about unobservables. Instrumentalists see theories as predictive tools without commitment to literal truth.

questions.text: Language meaning is determined by public use, not private intent.
questions.info: On the 'meaning is use' view, meaning depends on public practices and language games. Intention‑based accounts tie meaning to what speakers aim to communicate.

questions.text: Human actions are primarily driven by self-interest.
questions.info: Psychological egoism claims all actions are ultimately motivated by self‑interest. Critics argue genuine altruism and other‑regarding motives exist.

questions.text: The state may justly restrict liberty to promote overall welfare.
questions.info: This asks whether the state may restrict liberty to promote overall welfare. Utilitarians are open to trade‑offs; libertarians defend stringent side‑constraints on interference.

questions.text: Justice requires prioritizing the least advantaged.
questions.info: Rawls' difference principle prioritizes improving the position of the least advantaged. Critics prefer equality of opportunity, merit, or historical entitlement theories.

questions.text: Religious belief can be rational without evidence.
questions.info: Some hold religious belief can be rational without inferential evidence (properly basic). Evidentialists insist beliefs require sufficient evidence to be epistemically justified.

questions.text: God's existence is required for objective morality.
questions.info: Divine command theories ground objective morality in God's will. Alternatives ground morality in reason, human nature, or other secular foundations (see Euthyphro dilemma).

questions.text: Aesthetic value can be judged by objective standards.
questions.info: Objectivists about aesthetics think standards of taste track objective features. Subjectivists claim aesthetic value depends on individual or cultural responses.

questions.text: Moral rules admit no exceptions.
questions.info: Deontologists often defend exceptionless moral rules (e.g., against lying). Consequentialists argue rules should yield to best outcomes in particular cases.

questions.text: Historical progress follows discernible laws.
questions.info: Historicism posits that history unfolds according to discernible laws or stages. Skeptics argue grand predictive laws of history are illusory.

questions.text: Social reality is constructed by power relations.
questions.info: Social construction views hold that norms and categories are produced by social practices and power. Debate centers on the extent and mechanisms of construction versus underlying realities.

questions.text: Basic beliefs can be justified without inference.
questions.info: Foundationalism claims some basic beliefs are justified non‑inferentially and support others. Coherentists deny privileged foundations, stressing mutual support within a web of belief.

questions.text: Intuitions are reliable sources of philosophical evidence.
questions.info: Philosophical intuitions are pre‑theoretic judgments used as evidence in arguments and thought experiments. Critics question their reliability and cultural neutrality.

questions.text: We should maximize happiness rather than respect rules.
questions.info: Act‑utilitarianism says we should maximize happiness rather than follow fixed rules. Rule‑based and deontological views prioritize constraints or rights over aggregate welfare.

questions.text: Individuals have inalienable natural rights.
questions.info: Natural rights theories hold individuals possess inalienable rights prior to the state. Opponents treat rights as conventional, derived from social contracts or consequences.

questions.text: The mind has innate ideas.
questions.info: Innatism claims the mind contains innate ideas or structures that shape knowledge. Empiricists deny innate content, emphasizing learning from experience.

questions.text: Moral motivation is primarily driven by reason.
questions.info: This asks whether reason primarily motivates moral action. Sentimentalists give pride of place to emotions and desires, with reason guiding means.

questions.text: Truth is relative to conceptual schemes.
questions.info: Relativism holds truth depends on conceptual schemes or cultures. Realists argue many truths are objective and scheme‑independent.

questions.text: Meaning is use within language games.
questions.info: Meaning‑as‑use (language games) ties meaning to shared practices rather than mental representations. Debates concern private language, speaker intentions, and reference to the world.

questions.text: History is best explained by material conditions.
questions.info: Historical materialism explains social change chiefly by material conditions and modes of production. Rivals emphasize ideas, individuals, or contingencies over economic structures.

questions.text: The will to power underlies human striving.
questions.info: Nietzsche's 'will to power' frames human striving as the drive to create, assert, and overcome. It contrasts with moralizing frameworks and purely hedonistic psychologies.

philosophers.name: Plato
philosophers.justification: Plato's metaphysics centers on *Forms* as objective, rational, and independent of perception, underpinning his ethics (virtue > consequences), epistemology (innate knowledge via *anamnesis*), and politics (justice as harmony). His dualism (soul/body, *epistēmē/doxa*) rejects empiricism, materialism, and relativism, while his dialectic prioritizes reason over empirical or power-based constructs. Exceptions arise in social/political theory (e.g., flexible justice) or debates like language meaning, where conventional use intersects with *Forms*.

philosophers.name: Aristotle
philosophers.justification: Aristotle's philosophy centers on **teleology** (purpose-driven nature), **virtue ethics**, and **empirical realism**. He rejects consequentialism (Q1) and relativism (Q27), instead grounding morality in rational *arete* (excellence). Free will (Q3) is compatible with determinism via agency within natural laws, but he denies reductive physicalism (Q8) and innate ideas (Q25), emphasizing experience (Q5) tempered by reason (Q6). His politics (Q13) balances liberty and welfare but rejects modern egalitarianism (Q14). Aesthetics (Q17) and language (Q11, Q28) reflect objective structures, while history (Q19, Q29) is shaped by natural and cultural *telos*, not materialism or power (Q20, Q30). Religious claims (Q15–Q16) are secondary to philosophical inquiry.

philosophers.name: Aquinas
philosophers.justification: Aquinas' Thomism centers on **realism, rationalism, and teleology**. He rejects consequentialism (Q1), affirming objective moral truths grounded in divine law (Q2, Q16). While he accepts free will (Q3), his deterministic leanings (e.g., divine foreknowledge) create tension. The external world’s independence (Q4) and reason’s primacy (Q6) align with his epistemology, though he acknowledges sensory experience (Q5). Identity (Q7) and dualism (Q8) are nuanced—he leans toward hylomorphic unity but rejects reductive physicalism. Mathematical entities (Q9) and scientific truth-seeking (Q10) reflect his Aristotelian realism. Language (Q11) and self-interest (Q12) are secondary to divine order; justice (Q13–Q14) is tied to natural law. Religious belief (Q15) is rational via *via negativa* and reason. Aesthetics (Q17) and moral rules (Q18) admit divine teleology but not rigid exceptions. Progress (Q19) and social construction (Q20) are dismissed in favor of eternal truths. Innate ideas (Q25) and reason-driven morality (Q26) are central, while relativism (Q27–Q28), materialism (Q29), and voluntarism (Q30) are rejected. His views on basic beliefs (Q21) and intuitions (Q22) prioritize revelation and reason over empiricism.

philosophers.name: Descartes
philosophers.justification: Descartes' ratings reflect his dualist metaphysics, rationalist epistemology, and theological commitments. He rejects consequentialism (Q1), cultural relativism (Q2), and materialist determinism (Q3), while affirming the external world's existence (Q4) and *a priori* reason (Q6, Q21). His rejection of empiricism (Q5) and physicalism (Q8) aligns with innate ideas (Q25) and God's role in grounding morality (Q16). Passions overrule reason in motivation (Q26), and truth is absolute (Q27), contrasting with modern relativism. Political philosophy leans toward reasoned state authority (Q13) but rejects distributive justice (Q14) or materialist history (Q29).

philosophers.name: Hume
philosophers.justification: Hume's empiricism and skepticism shape his ratings: he rejects innate ideas (Q25), objective morality (Q2, Q14, Q16), and reason's independent power (Q6, Q26), favoring sensory experience (Q5), utility (Q1, Q23), and public language use (Q11, Q28). His compatibilism on free will (Q3) and skepticism about identity (Q7) reflect his causal empiricism. While he acknowledges material conditions in history (Q29), he resists grand narratives (Q19, Q30) and power-based social construction (Q20) as absolute. His moderate stance on state authority (Q13) contrasts with rigid moral rules (Q18) or natural rights (Q24).

philosophers.name: Kant
philosophers.justification: Kant's ratings reflect his transcendental idealism, deontological ethics, and rationalism. He rejects consequentialism (Q1), relativism (Q27), and materialism (Q8, Q29), while affirming objective morality (Q2), a priori knowledge (Q6, Q21), and autonomy (Q24, Q26). His critique of innate ideas (Q25) and empirical foundations (Q5) aligns with his epistemological stance. Social and political views prioritize reason over power (Q20) or self-interest (Q12), and his aesthetics (Q17) and ethics (Q18) emphasize universality. Exceptions like Q3 (free will) and Q19 (progress) reflect unresolved tensions in his system.

philosophers.name: Spinoza
philosophers.justification: Spinoza's system is a *rationalist* monism where God/Nature (*Deus sive Natura*) is the sole substance. Moral truths are objective and derived from eternal laws, not culture or consequences. Free will is reconceived as rational comprehension of necessity, rejecting both libertarianism and hard determinism. The mind-body problem is resolved via *parallelism*: mental states are physical modes of one substance. Knowledge is either *sensory* (limited) or *intellectual* (certain, via intuition). Politics (*TTP*) critiques absolutism, emphasizing natural rights and the social contract's necessity. Aesthetic and moral values reflect harmony with Nature's order, but exceptions to rules are permitted by reason. History and society are governed by material conditions, yet ultimate explanations lie in metaphysical necessity. His ethics prioritizes *virtue* (rational love of God) over happiness or power.

philosophers.name: Nietzsche
philosophers.justification: Nietzsche’s thought rejects utilitarianism (Q1), objectivism (Q2, Q14, Q16), and rationalist epistemology (Q6, Q10, Q21). He critiques free will as an illusion (Q3) and denies persistent identity (Q7), aligning with materialism (Q8) but rejecting innate ideas (Q25). His perspectivism (Q27) and anti-realism (Q9) inform his skepticism of objective standards (Q17, Q18). Nietzsche’s *will to power* (Q30) drives his critique of morality (Q12, Q23), history (Q19, Q29), and social constructs (Q20), while valuing aesthetic and linguistic pragmatism (Q11, Q28). His skepticism of reason (Q26) and evidence-based belief (Q15) contrasts with his affirmation of intuitive, creative knowledge (Q22).

philosophers.name: J. S. Mill
philosophers.justification: Mill’s utilitarianism grounds moral rightness in consequences (Q1:5), rejecting rigid objectivism (Q2:3) while allowing cultural relativity. He aligns free will with determinism via compatibilism (Q3:4) and defends empirical realism (Q4:5). Knowledge arises from experience (Q5:4) but also reason (Q6:4). Identity is psychological (Q7:3), and he rejects physicalism (Q8:2). Mathematical truths are conventional (Q9:3), while science seeks truth (Q10:5). Language meaning is use-driven (Q11:4), but he critiques self-interest (Q12:2) and defends liberal restrictions (Q13:5). Justice for Mill prioritizes welfare, not rigid equality (Q14:3). He dismisses religious morality (Q15:1, Q16:1) and aesthetic objectivism (Q17:2). Moral rules admit exceptions (Q18:2), and progress is empirical (Q19:4). Social reality is shaped by institutions, not just power (Q20:3). Basic beliefs need justification (Q21:3), and intuitions are fallible (Q22:2). Happiness maximization trumps rigid rules (Q23:5), and rights are instrumental (Q24:3). He rejects innate ideas (Q25:1) and emotional over reason in morality (Q26:1). Truth is not relativist (Q27:3), but meaning is use-based (Q28:4). Material conditions matter (Q29:3), but power is secondary (Q30:1).

philosophers.name: Marx
philosophers.justification: Marx's philosophy centers on **historical materialism**, rejecting idealist abstractions (e.g., innate ideas, divine morality) in favor of **material conditions** shaping reality, knowledge, and ethics. He aligns with **empiricism** (Q5, Q8) and **reductive physicalism** (Q4, Q8), but critiques **utilitarianism** (Q1, Q23) as class-biased. **Justice** (Q14, Q20) is tied to proletarian revolution, not abstract rights (Q24). **Language** (Q11, Q28) and **science** (Q10, Q29) serve material transformation. **Relativism** (Q27) is rejected; truth is objective but historically contingent. **Free will** (Q3) is denied in favor of deterministic class structures, while **aesthetics** (Q17) and **morality** (Q16) are materially grounded. Nietzsche's *will to power* (Q30) is dismissed as idealist.

philosophers.name: Kierkegaard
philosophers.justification: Kierkegaard's thought centers on *subjective* truth, *faith*, and *existential* choice, rejecting both rationalism and empiricism. He critiques objectivism (Q2, Q9, Q14) and materialism (Q8, Q29), while affirming the primacy of *individual* moral and religious experience (Q15, Q21, Q22). His ethics prioritize *duty* and *passion* over consequences (Q1, Q23) or self-interest (Q12), and he views identity (Q7) and truth (Q6, Q27) as teleological, not static. While he engages with culture (Q2, Q11), his focus on the *leap of faith* (Q15, Q16) and the *sincerity* of belief (Q21) sets him apart from both naturalism and relativism.

philosophers.justification: Wittgenstein's later work (*Philosophical Investigations*, *On Certainty*) rejects foundationalist, metaphysical, and essentialist claims, emphasizing *language games* as the locus of meaning, truth, and morality. His *Tractatus* posits a rigid fact-value distinction and logical atomism, but later critiques these as confusions. philosophers.name: Russell
philosophers.justification: Bertrand Russell was a logical empiricist and pluralist who emphasized scientific method, skepticism toward metaphysics, and a commitment to objective knowledge. He rejected moral relativism (Q1, Q2) but allowed limited compatibilism on free will (Q3). His empiricism clashed with innate ideas (Q25) and mathematical realism (Q9), while his pragmatism aligned with truth-as-usefulness (Q10). He criticized absolute moral rules (Q18) and religious foundations for morality (Q15–Q17), but defended natural rights (Q24) and social welfare (Q13). His later work leaned toward neutralist ethics (Q23) and rejected metaphysical constructs like the will to power (Q30).

philosophers.name: Rawls
philosophers.justification: Rawls' work centers on *justice as fairness*, a Kantian-inspired framework prioritizing impartiality, rights, and the least advantaged. He rejects consequentialism (Q1) and divine foundations for morality (Q16), instead deriving objective principles via reflective equilibrium (Q6, Q21–22). While he assumes a realist ontology (Q4, Q14), his epistemology leans toward reasoned principles over empiricism (Q5) or innatism (Q25). Social cooperation (Q12–13) is framed by mutual advantage, not power (Q30) or pure self-interest. His later work (*Political Liberalism*) acknowledges pluralism but retains objectivity in overlapping consensus (Q2, Q27). Rights (Q24) and the difference principle (Q14) are non-negotiable, though he allows contextual flexibility (Q18). Science and reason (Q10, Q26) ground justice, but he avoids reductionism (Q8, Q29) or radical constructivism (Q20).

philosophers.name: Foucault
philosophers.justification: Foucault’s work critiques foundationalist, universalist, and essentialist claims (Q1–Q19), rejecting objective moral truths (Q2), innate ideas (Q25), and fixed identities (Q7–Q8). He emphasizes power’s role in shaping knowledge (Q20, Q30) and language (Q11, Q28), aligning with anti-realist views on truth (Q27) and materialist explanations of history (Q29). His later work (*The Care of the Self*) suggests ethical practices emerge from discursive formations, not self-interest (Q12) or reason alone (Q6, Q26). Foucault’s skepticism toward progress (Q19), natural rights (Q24), and religious rationalism (Q15–Q17) is well-documented, while his focus on *will to power* (Q30) and *language games* (Q28) reflects Nietzschean and post-structuralist influences. Neutral/mixed ratings (Q3, Q13–Q14) reflect his nuanced views on state power and justice as contingent, not absolute.
